01 / 12 ? for help
Whitehall Reply × LUISS · Project 2 · 2025/26

Transit Anomaly Detection

Classical pipeline vs Multi-agent architecture — a comparative study on Italian airport border-control data.
Giulio D'Amico  ·  Alexis Mitracos
1,100 routes  ·  4 detectors  ·  5 agents  ·  1 shared core
Transit Anomaly Detection 02 / 12

From reactive to proactive

Why a comparative study, not a winner declaration.
  • Border-control authorities process thousands of transits per day — timestamps, gates, routes, nationalities, document types, security alerts.
  • Today, anomaly detection is largely reactive: incidents are reviewed after the fact.
  • A proactive analytical layer would surface suspicious patterns before they escalate into operational risks or security incidents.
Project ask

Build the same anomaly-detection system twice — classical statistical pipeline and multi-agent architecture — and produce a comparative analysis to argue which approach fits which operational regime.

Transit Anomaly Detection 03 / 12

Data — two CSVs, real-world dirty

Italian airport border-control aggregates, 2-month window.
Dataset Rows Cols Observation unit
TIPOLOGIA_VIAGGIATORE 5,095 33 Daily traveler-category aggregates per departure airport
ALLARMI 5,080 24 Alarm-event aggregates per (airport × month × occurrence type)

Cleaning challenges

Schema noise40+ Italian column names, mixed casing, typos (AREOPORTO instead of AEROPORTO).
Date parsingSix different date formats coexist in the same column. Solved with format='mixed' + fallback pass.
PlaceholdersN.D., ??, //, xx, zz → unified controlled vocabulary.
Signal cap [0, 200]Keeps >99.5% of legitimate values, removes parsing artefacts and free-text leakage.
Transit Anomaly Detection 04 / 12

One core, two pipelines

A single module owns cleaning, features, detection, and post-processing — both pipelines import from it.
Classical
deterministic notebook
utils.py
cleaning · features · detection · post-processing
Multi-agent
LangGraph orchestrator

Any divergence between pipeline outputs reflects architectural behaviour, never algorithmic drift. Parity is guaranteed by construction.

→ This design choice is what makes the §4.2 Jaccard agreement (slide 10) a meaningful test, not a tautology.

Transit Anomaly Detection 05 / 12

Feature engineering at route level

One row per (departure_iata, arrival_iata) route — three feature families.
Volume
  • tot_entries
  • tot_investigated
  • tot_alarms
  • Pivoted occurrence counts
Rate
  • alert_rate (capped at 1.0)
  • investigation_rate
  • Segmented per top-3 nationalities, doc types, control outcomes
Risk
  • n_high_risk from RISK_FLAG
  • n_medium_risk from RISK_FLAG
Transform log1p over count features (heavy right tails) → StandardScaler before detection

→ Density inspection and correlation matrix in appendix (press A). Segmented rates introduce horizontal information that single global rates would hide.

Transit Anomaly Detection 06 / 12

Four detectors, one consensus

Deliberately different inductive biases — a route is anomalous if ≥ 2 of 4 agree.
Isolation Forest
Global random partitioning
0 flags
LOF
Local density vs neighbours
0 flags
DBSCAN
Density connectivity
0 flags
Robust Z-score
Per-feature tail (median + MAD)
0 flags
Consensus rule

A route is flagged anomalous if ≥ 2 of 4 detectors agree: 1,100 routes → 41 consensus → 33 after QA filter.

→ The QA filter excludes routes with incomplete data, likely false positives on non-rate features, or rates measured on ≤ 3 transits (kept with warning).

Transit Anomaly Detection 07 / 12

Two non-trivial methodological calls

Off-the-shelf rules don't fit our regime — both decisions documented with empirical justification.

① DBSCAN min_samples in high-dim

Textbook rule min_samples = 2·d (Sander et al.) with d ≈ 30 features over ≈ 1,100 routes labelled ~75% of the dataset as noise — DBSCAN became inert.
↓
Adopted high-dim practical rule max(5, ⌈ln(n)⌉ + 1) → noise rate ~1.5%. DBSCAN now isolates a small number of structurally distant points — the operational target.

② Robust Z-score over vanilla Z

Vanilla (x − mean) / std on the standardised matrix flagged ~20% of routes (215 / 1,100) — dominating the consensus vote by construction.
↓
Replaced with (x − median) / MAD (median absolute deviation). Flag count dropped to ~60–90 routes: the four detectors are now genuinely complementary.
Transit Anomaly Detection 08 / 12

Wilson CI guards against tiny-volume false positives

100% alert rate on 1 transit ≠ 100% alert rate on 50 — the priority score must reflect this.

Alert rate vs investigated volume — Pearson r = −0.020 over the full panel.

Priority score
priority = (0.60·rate_n  +  0.40·log_alarms_n) × ci_tightness
  • rate_n, alarms_n are MinMax-normalised to [0, 1].
  • ci_tightness = 1 − ci95_width — downweights routes with sparse evidence.
  • Wilson 95% CI is computed on the alert rate; the CI half-width captures the difference between large and small samples.
Transit Anomaly Detection 09 / 12

Multi-agent: same core, different control flow

Five specialist agents orchestrated by LangGraph. State is append-only; LLM scope is deliberately minimal.
Data
Parse free-text
request → perimeter
Baseline
Aggregate at route level
build feature matrix
Outlier
Run 4-detector
ensemble + consensus
Risk
Risk levels + Wilson CI
+ priority + QA
Report
Markdown report
+ interpretation
conditional edge after Data: empty / invalid perimeter → skip detection → graceful Report
LLM is invoked at exactly 2 points

(1) Parsing free-text request into a structured perimeter (with deterministic IATA / city / country fallbacks first).
(2) Writing one 4-sentence interpretation paragraph in the report.

Robustness

If the LLM call fails, the report renders with a generic interpretation paragraph. The deliverable is never blocked by LLM availability.

Tested across 5 perimeters (IATA, city, country, invalid IATA, junk) — graceful in all cases.

Transit Anomaly Detection 10 / 12

The proof: same output, agentic interface for free

0
Consensus anomalies after QA filter
1 CRITICAL · 16 HIGH · 12 MEDIUM · 4 LOW
0%
Jaccard agreement
classical ↔ multi-agent on full perimeter
+0%
Latency overhead
0.481s → 0.501s · bounded by 1–2 LLM calls
Transit Anomaly Detection 11 / 12

When to use which

The two pipelines are mathematically equivalent — the choice is a deployment choice.
Scenario Classical Multi-Agent
Scheduled batch report, fixed perimeter, archived for audit✓
Bit-identical reproducibility (no LLM dependency)✓
Latency-critical scoring (sub-second)✓
Explainability before a regulator / data-protection authority✓
Low operational cost (no GPU, no LLM tokens)✓
Analyst exploration on arbitrary, ad-hoc perimeters✓
Stakeholder-ready narrative output without manual editing✓
Free-text / multilingual input from non-technical operators✓
Graceful failure on missing / invalid input✓
Pipeline must extend to new specialist roles (geo-agent, feedback-agent)✓
Recommendation: deploy both. Classical as the nightly system-of-record. Multi-agent as the on-demand exploration layer above it.
Transit Anomaly Detection 12 / 12

What this work cannot answer (yet)

Honest limitations and the natural extensions.

Limitations

  • No ground-truth labels. Without operator-labelled anomalies, neither pipeline can be evaluated for precision or recall — Jaccard is internal-consistency, not accuracy.
  • LLM non-determinism. Even with temperature=0 and fixed seed, minor variations remain in the narrative paragraph. Numbers, rankings, risk levels are unaffected.
  • Single 2-month window. No strict temporal back-testing possible (rolling baselines, train-on-month-1 / score-on-month-2).
  • Auto-detected segmentation. Top-3 nationality / doc / outcome categories are computed from the data — should be frozen per release in production.

Next steps

  • Operator-labelled validation set → real precision / recall per risk level.
  • Temporal baselines → rolling 7-day and 28-day rates per route to flag changes in behaviour, not just absolute outliers.
  • Geo-agent for country / region clustering, paired with the existing risk agent.
  • Feedback-agent ingesting operator decisions back into ranking weights — closes the loop between detection and triage.

Univariate density — heavy right tails on counts justify log1p.

MAD-modified Z-score: 23 routes flagged on tot_investigated and n_records.
